quiz_questions.question_text: Where does most gas exchange between the atmosphere and ocean occur?

options.text: At the deep ocean floor
At the ocean surface
At hydrothermal vents
In underwater caves

quiz_questions.explanation: Gas exchange between the atmosphere and ocean primarily occurs at the ocean surface, where air and water are in direct contact. Wind and waves increase the surface area, promoting more efficient gas transfer.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does water temperature affect the solubility of oxygen in seawater?

options.text: Warmer water holds more dissolved oxygen
Temperature has no effect on oxygen solubility
Colder water holds more dissolved oxygen
Only tropical water can dissolve oxygen

quiz_questions.explanation: Cold water can hold more dissolved oxygen than warm water because gas molecules move more slowly at lower temperatures and are more easily retained in the liquid. This is why polar waters tend to be more oxygen-rich than tropical waters.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the primary biological source of dissolved oxygen in the ocean?

options.text: Decomposition of organic matter
Photosynthesis by phytoplankton and marine plants
Respiration by fish
Chemical reactions with sea salts

quiz_questions.explanation: Photosynthesis by phytoplankton and other marine plants produces the majority of dissolved oxygen in the ocean. These organisms use sunlight and CO₂ to make organic compounds, releasing O₂ as a byproduct.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which process removes dissolved oxygen from seawater?

options.text: Photosynthesis
Evaporation of water
Respiration by marine organisms
Freezing of sea ice

quiz_questions.explanation: Aerobic respiration by marine organisms, from bacteria to whales, consumes dissolved oxygen. Decomposition of dead organic matter by bacteria also uses large amounts of oxygen, particularly in deeper waters.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to carbon dioxide when it dissolves in seawater?

options.text: It remains as dissolved CO₂ gas only
It reacts with water to form carbonic acid, bicarbonate, and carbonate ions
It immediately escapes back into the atmosphere
It bonds with sodium chloride

quiz_questions.explanation: When CO₂ dissolves in seawater, it reacts with water to form carbonic acid (H₂CO₃), which then dissociates into bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻) and carbonate (CO₃²⁻) ions. This series of reactions is central to the ocean's carbon chemistry.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which zone of the ocean typically has the highest concentration of dissolved oxygen?

options.text: The abyssal zone
The surface mixed layer
The oxygen minimum zone
The hadal zone

quiz_questions.explanation: The surface mixed layer has the highest dissolved oxygen because it is in direct contact with the atmosphere and receives oxygen from photosynthesis. Wind and wave action help mix oxygen into this layer.

quiz_questions.question_text: What role does wind play in ocean gas exchange?

options.text: Wind removes all gases from the ocean surface
Wind has no effect on gas exchange
Wind increases gas exchange by creating waves and turbulence at the surface
Wind only affects temperature, not gas exchange

quiz_questions.explanation: Wind increases gas exchange by generating waves and turbulence that break the ocean's surface layer, increasing the area of air-water contact. Higher wind speeds lead to greater rates of gas transfer between the atmosphere and ocean.

quiz_questions.question_text: Approximately what percentage of global oxygen production comes from marine photosynthesis?

options.text: About 10%
About 25%
About 50% or more
About 90%

quiz_questions.explanation: Marine photosynthesis, primarily by phytoplankton, produces approximately 50% or more of the world's oxygen. This makes the ocean one of the most important sources of atmospheric oxygen on Earth.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does salinity affect the solubility of gases in seawater?

options.text: Higher salinity increases gas solubility
Higher salinity decreases gas solubility
Salinity has no effect on gas solubility
Salinity only affects CO₂ solubility, not O₂

quiz_questions.explanation: Higher salinity decreases the solubility of gases in water due to the "salting out" effect. Dissolved ions occupy space and interact with water molecules, reducing the water's capacity to hold dissolved gases like O₂ and CO₂.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the ocean's biological pump?

options.text: A machine used by scientists to sample deep water
The process by which surface organisms fix carbon and transport it to the deep ocean
The circulation of warm water to the poles
The release of oxygen from hydrothermal vents

quiz_questions.explanation: The biological pump is the process by which phytoplankton fix CO₂ through photosynthesis and the resulting organic carbon is transported to deeper waters when organisms die, sink, or are consumed. This moves carbon from the surface to the deep ocean.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does "gas solubility" mean?

options.text: The rate at which gas bubbles rise through water
The maximum amount of a gas that can dissolve in a given volume of liquid under specific conditions
The speed at which gas escapes from water
The temperature at which a gas becomes liquid

quiz_questions.explanation: Gas solubility is the maximum concentration of a gas that can dissolve in a liquid at a given temperature, pressure, and salinity. It determines how much O₂ or CO₂ seawater can hold under specific environmental conditions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the "oxygen minimum zone" (OMZ)?

options.text: A surface layer where photosynthesis produces maximum oxygen
A mid-depth layer where dissolved oxygen is at its lowest due to respiration and decomposition
The deepest part of the ocean where no oxygen exists
An artificial zone created by pollution

quiz_questions.explanation: The oxygen minimum zone (OMZ) is a layer typically found between 200–1000 meters depth where dissolved oxygen concentrations are at their lowest. This occurs because respiration and decomposition consume oxygen, while distance from the surface limits replenishment.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is "Henry's Law" as it applies to ocean gas exchange?

options.text: The rate of gas exchange is independent of wind speed
The amount of gas that dissolves in a liquid is proportional to the partial pressure of that gas above the liquid
Gases always move from the ocean to the atmosphere
Temperature does not affect gas dissolution

quiz_questions.explanation: Henry's Law states that at a constant temperature, the amount of gas dissolved in a liquid is directly proportional to the partial pressure of that gas in the atmosphere above the liquid. Higher atmospheric CO₂ levels therefore lead to more CO₂ dissolving in the ocean.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does "partial pressure" of a gas mean?

options.text: The total atmospheric pressure at sea level
The pressure exerted by a single gas in a mixture of gases
The pressure at a certain depth underwater
The reduction of pressure during a storm

quiz_questions.explanation: Partial pressure is the pressure contribution of an individual gas in a mixture of gases. For example, the partial pressure of CO₂ (pCO₂) in the atmosphere determines how much CO₂ will dissolve into the ocean surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is "diffusion" in the context of gas exchange?

options.text: The process of water evaporating from the ocean
The movement of gas molecules from an area of higher concentration to lower concentration
The sinking of cold water to the ocean floor
The reflection of sunlight off the ocean surface

quiz_questions.explanation: Diffusion is the net movement of molecules from a region of higher concentration to a region of lower concentration. In ocean gas exchange, if the surface water has less dissolved CO₂ than the atmosphere, CO₂ will diffuse from air into the water.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is "respiration" in the marine environment?

options.text: The process by which organisms use oxygen and produce carbon dioxide to release energy from food
The absorption of sunlight by algae
The formation of ocean currents
The evaporation of water from the ocean surface

quiz_questions.explanation: Respiration is a metabolic process where organisms consume oxygen and organic compounds to produce energy, releasing CO₂ and water as byproducts. In the ocean, respiration by all marine life forms is a major consumer of dissolved oxygen.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term "carbon sink" refer to?

options.text: A region that releases more carbon than it absorbs
A natural reservoir that absorbs and stores more carbon than it releases
A type of deep sea trench
A measurement of ocean salinity

quiz_questions.explanation: A carbon sink is a natural system that absorbs more carbon dioxide from the atmosphere than it releases. The ocean is the largest active carbon sink on Earth, absorbing roughly 25-30% of human-produced CO₂ annually.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is "supersaturation" of a dissolved gas?

options.text: When a gas is completely absent from water
When the concentration of dissolved gas is below equilibrium
When the concentration of dissolved gas exceeds the equilibrium value for current conditions
When gas cannot dissolve due to high salinity

quiz_questions.explanation: Supersaturation occurs when the dissolved gas concentration exceeds what the water can hold at equilibrium under current temperature and pressure conditions. This can happen when water warms rapidly or during intense photosynthesis, and excess gas tends to escape to the atmosphere.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term "ventilation" mean in physical oceanography?

options.text: The warming of surface water by solar radiation
The process by which surface water carrying dissolved gases sinks and supplies oxygen to deeper layers
The creation of tidal pools
The mixing of fresh and salt water in estuaries

quiz_questions.explanation: Ventilation in oceanography refers to the process by which surface water, enriched with atmospheric gases, is transported to the ocean interior through sinking or mixing. This is the primary way deep ocean waters receive dissolved oxygen.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is "equilibrium" in the context of air-sea gas exchange?

options.text: The point at which all gas has escaped from the water
When the rate of gas dissolving into water equals the rate of gas escaping to the atmosphere
When ocean temperature equals atmospheric temperature
When waves and wind completely stop

quiz_questions.explanation: Equilibrium in air-sea gas exchange occurs when the flux of gas molecules entering the water equals the flux leaving it, resulting in no net transfer. At equilibrium, the dissolved gas concentration is determined by the gas's partial pressure and solubility.

quiz_questions.question_text: A marine biologist observes a massive phytoplankton bloom in a bay. How would this bloom most likely affect dissolved oxygen levels during the daytime?

options.text: Dissolved oxygen would decrease significantly
Dissolved oxygen would increase due to photosynthesis
Dissolved oxygen would remain unaffected
Dissolved oxygen would be converted to nitrogen

quiz_questions.explanation: During the day, a large phytoplankton bloom carries out intense photosynthesis, producing substantial amounts of dissolved oxygen. The water can even become supersaturated with oxygen. However, at night when photosynthesis stops, respiration can cause oxygen to drop significantly.

quiz_questions.question_text: A scientist finds that a deep ocean basin has almost no dissolved oxygen. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: Too much photosynthesis is occurring at depth
Bacterial decomposition of sinking organic matter has consumed the available oxygen
The water is too cold to hold dissolved oxygen
Wind is mixing oxygen out of the deep water

quiz_questions.explanation: In poorly ventilated deep basins, bacteria decompose sinking organic matter (dead organisms, fecal pellets) through respiration, consuming dissolved oxygen. Without adequate mixing or circulation to replenish oxygen from the surface, the water becomes anoxic.

quiz_questions.question_text: Atmospheric CO₂ levels have risen from 280 ppm to over 420 ppm since the Industrial Revolution. According to Henry's Law, how has this affected the ocean?

options.text: The ocean has absorbed less CO₂ because warmer temperatures decrease solubility
The ocean has absorbed more CO₂ because higher atmospheric pCO₂ increases dissolution
The ocean's CO₂ content is independent of atmospheric levels
The ocean has released CO₂ in response to rising atmospheric levels

quiz_questions.explanation: According to Henry's Law, increasing the partial pressure of CO₂ in the atmosphere increases the amount that dissolves in the ocean surface. The ocean has absorbed roughly 30% of anthropogenic CO₂ emissions, leading to measurable changes in ocean chemistry.

quiz_questions.question_text: A coastal region experiences upwelling, bringing deep, cold water to the surface. What would you predict about dissolved CO₂ in the upwelled water?

options.text: It would contain very little dissolved CO₂
It would be rich in dissolved CO₂ from deep-water respiration and decomposition
Upwelling has no effect on CO₂ concentrations
It would contain only dissolved oxygen

quiz_questions.explanation: Deep ocean water accumulates CO₂ from the respiration and decomposition of organic matter over time. When this water is brought to the surface through upwelling, it carries elevated dissolved CO₂ levels, which can be released to the atmosphere.

quiz_questions.question_text: A fish farmer notices large fish die-offs in a warm, stagnant pond during summer. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The water became too saline for the fish
Low dissolved oxygen due to warm temperatures and high biological oxygen demand
Too much sunlight damaged the fish directly
Excessive dissolved oxygen poisoned the fish

quiz_questions.explanation: Warm water holds less dissolved oxygen, and stagnant water has little circulation to replenish oxygen from the atmosphere. Combined with high respiration rates from bacteria and other organisms in summer, dissolved oxygen can drop to lethal levels for fish.

quiz_questions.question_text: During a storm, large waves crash along the coast. How does this event affect gas exchange?

options.text: Gas exchange decreases because the water is disturbed
Gas exchange increases because wave action and bubbles increase air-water contact
Gas exchange stops completely during storms
Only CO₂ exchange is affected, not O₂

quiz_questions.explanation: Storms significantly increase gas exchange rates. Breaking waves inject air bubbles below the surface and create spray above, both of which dramatically increase the surface area for air-water contact and enhance dissolution of atmospheric gases.

quiz_questions.question_text: A limnologist compares gas exchange in a freshwater lake and a nearby ocean bay with similar temperatures. Which body of water would hold more dissolved oxygen?

options.text: The ocean bay because salt enhances oxygen solubility
Both would hold the same amount
The freshwater lake because lower salinity allows greater gas solubility
Neither can hold dissolved oxygen at the same temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: At the same temperature, freshwater can hold more dissolved oxygen than saltwater due to the salting-out effect. Dissolved ions in seawater reduce the water's capacity to dissolve gases, meaning the freshwater lake would have higher oxygen solubility.

quiz_questions.question_text: A research vessel measures high pCO₂ in surface water at a tropical upwelling zone. What will likely happen to the CO₂?

options.text: It will sink to the deep ocean
It will be absorbed by nearby land plants
The excess CO₂ will degas from the ocean surface into the atmosphere
It will be immediately converted to oxygen

quiz_questions.explanation: When surface ocean water has a higher pCO₂ than the atmosphere, the gas flux is from sea to air—the ocean releases CO₂. Upwelling zones are often sources of CO₂ to the atmosphere because the upwelled deep water is enriched in CO₂.

quiz_questions.question_text: If global warming causes ocean surface temperatures to rise by 2°C, what combined effect would this have on dissolved oxygen and CO₂?

options.text: Both dissolved O₂ and CO₂ solubility would increase
Both dissolved O₂ and CO₂ solubility would decrease
O₂ solubility would increase while CO₂ solubility decreases
Temperature has no effect on gas solubility

quiz_questions.explanation: Warmer water has reduced solubility for all gases. A 2°C rise in ocean surface temperature would decrease the capacity of seawater to hold both dissolved oxygen and CO₂. This creates a positive feedback loop where the warming ocean absorbs less CO₂ and holds less O₂.

quiz_questions.question_text: A coral reef is located in warm, shallow water near a nutrient-rich runoff area. The reef experiences nighttime oxygen crashes. What is the best explanation?

options.text: Corals produce excess oxygen at night
Only daytime light can affect dissolved gas levels
Nutrient runoff fuels algal growth whose nighttime respiration depletes oxygen in warm water with already low O₂ capacity
The reef consumes oxygen to build its limestone skeleton

quiz_questions.explanation: Nutrient-rich runoff promotes algal blooms. During the day, algae produce oxygen via photosynthesis, but at night, all organisms respire without photosynthetic oxygen production. In warm water with reduced O₂ capacity, this can lead to dangerously low oxygen levels.
